title: The Original Bonfire of the Vanities - History Today
highlights: 1497, the Piagnoni of Florence set sin ablaze in the original ‘bonfire of the vanities’  ...    ‘Piagnoni’, they were called: ‘weepers’. They, like the city, were under the sway of Girolamo Savonarola, a Dominican friar who  ...    Carnival in Florence was a time of wantonness and riot; under Savonarola it became an orderly ritual of destruction, culminating on 7 February 1497 with what is remembered as the bonfire of the vanities. Piagnoni went door to door demanding the surrender of sinful objects. One witness listed what they took: ‘lascivious  ...    Everything was brought to the Piazza della Signoria, where a great tiered wooden edifice had been built, somewhat like a pyramid, filled with brushwood and broom. At the structure’s apex stood a statue of Satan; demons circled its base. The goods were heaped on the pyre. Trump

title: February 7, 1497: The Bonfire of the Vanities - History and Headlines
highlights: A Brief History On February 7, 1497, the followers of Dominican priest Girolamo Savonarola of Florence, Italy, gathered and burned a large quantity of objects they referred to as “vanities,” objects such as cosmetics, books, artwork, mirrors, fancy clothes, playing cards, and musical instruments, any objects these religious zealots thought could lead people to sin.  ...
